coredata.dc:title: Humidity-enhanced NH3 sensor based on carbon quantum dots-modified SnS
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Yuxiang
Liu, Xiaoxuan
Xie, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Recently, SnS is been extensively investigated in the field of gas sensors due to its unique wrinkled honeycomb structure and high specific surface. However, the weak response to NH3 at room temperature and especially poor moisture resistance limit the application of SnS in high humidity environments. To overcome these challenges, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface were employed to modify SnS in this work, and the nanocomposite of SnS/CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal and stirring methods, respectively. It is revealed that the hydrothermally prepared nanocomposite SnS/CQDs shows much higher density of CQDs attached to the SnS nanosheets compared to the stirred samples. Resultantly, the hydrothermally prepared SnS/CQDs sensor can sense NH3 in ppb level and has a 4.78-fold higher response to 10 ppm NH3 than pure SnS, which are attributed to the heterogeneous structure of SnS/CQDs. Notably, its response value to 2.5 ppm NH3 at 97 % RH (3.67) is 1.44 times higher than that at 25 % RH, showing a unique humidity-enhanced effect. It was further explained by combining first-principles calculations and humidity-boosted NH3 adsorption-ionization model. This study provides a feasible way to explore the application potential of SnS-based nanocomposites in high humidity environments.

head.ce:title: Humidity-enhanced NH3 sensor based on carbon quantum dots-modified SnS

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, SnS is been extensively investigated in the field of gas sensors due to its unique wrinkled honeycomb structure and high specific surface. However, the weak response to NH3 at room temperature and especially poor moisture resistance limit the application of SnS in high humidity environments. To overcome these challenges, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface were employed to modify SnS in this work, and the nanocomposite of SnS/CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal and stirring methods, respectively. It is revealed that the hydrothermally prepared nanocomposite SnS/CQDs shows much higher density of CQDs attached to the SnS nanosheets compared to the stirred samples. Resultantly, the hydrothermally prepared SnS/CQDs sensor can sense NH3 in ppb level and has a 4.78-fold higher response to 10 ppm NH3 than pure SnS, which are attributed to the heterogeneous structure of SnS/CQDs. Notably, its response value to 2.5 ppm NH3 at 97 % RH (3.67) is 1.44 times higher than that at 25 % RH, showing a unique humidity-enhanced effect. It was further explained by combining first-principles calculations and humidity-boosted NH3 adsorption-ionization model. This study provides a feasible way to explore the application potential of SnS-based nanocomposites in high humidity environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3), a toxic gas with an irritating odor, is one of the most important materials widely present in agriculture, the chemical industry and fertilizer plants [1,2]. Excessive nitrogen emissions from agricultural activities can not only pollute the environment, but also affect the balance of agroecosystems and biodiversity [3,4]. Notably, long-term exposure to even low concentrations of ammonia can damage several organs of the body and even lead to death [5]. In addition, several clinical analyses [6,7] have found that metabolism in the body produces trace amounts of NH3, and the concentration of exhaled ammonia in humans is closely related to the health status of the kidneys. The average concentration of exhaled NH3 in patients with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) is about 4.88 ppm, which is higher than that of healthy individuals (0.96 ppm) [8]. Most currently reported gas sensors for high humidity environments (e.g., SnO2 [9–11], ZnO [12,13], and WO3 [14–16]) operate under high-temperature conditions, resulting in additional high-energy consumption. In addition, the agricultural environment and exhaled gas contain a large amount of water vapor [17,18], which can easily deteriorate the performance of the gas sensor. Therefore, it is of practical interest to develop gas sensors with low detection limits and reduced power consumption to detect NH3 in high humidity environments.

Recently, many two-dimensional (2D) semiconductor materials, such as graphene [19–21], black phosphorus (BP) [22], and MXene [23–25], have been extensively developed as room-temperature gas sensing materials. Among them, stannous sulfide (SnS) [26,27], a two-dimensional layered Group IV sulfide, has attracted attention due to its unique anisotropic wrinkled honeycomb structure and room-temperature NH3 sensitivity. However, the low response value and poor selectivity of SnS at room temperature, especially the unsatisfactory moisture resistance, make SnS need further modification. Adding hydrophobic materials on the material surface for modification is a common strategy for moisture resistance. However, the gas-sensitive sites on the surface may be occupied by hydrophobic materials, which may affect the performance of the sensor. For example, Jia et al. [28] deposited hydrophobic PFDS on the surface of ZnSnO3, so that the response values of the composite to 100 ppm ethanol at 80 % RH were almost the same as those in the dry state. Nevertheless, the response value of PFDS/ZnSnO3 is much lower than that of pure ZnSnO3, due to the hydrophobic PFDS covering the active sites on the ZnSnO3 surface. Therefore, it is required to find new modification methods that enable the composites to operate in high humidity environments while remaining highly sensitive to target gas.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [29–31], a novel 0-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, has the advantages of low toxicity, easy synthesis, and good water solubility. Compared with other carbonaceous nanomaterials [32,33] (like carbon nanotubes, graphene and MXene), CQDs exhibits fascinating advantages for gas sensors due to its extremely small size, abundant functional groups and unique electron transfer capability. Cheng and his group [34] prepared litchi-like In2O3/CQDs by hydrothermal method to achieve the detection of 50 ppb NO2. As demonstrated, CQDs not only possessed highly chemically active functional groups on its surfaces to facilitate NO2 adsorption, but also could act as electron donors to provide more electrons for adsorption of NO2. In addition, Marilena. Claimed [35] that the composites obtained by dispersing CQDs on the surface of NiO via mechanical stirring had stable CH4-sensing properties in high humidity environments. They concluded that the heterogeneous synergistic effect between CQDs and NiO promoted the oxidation reaction of CH4, demonstrating the excellent potential of CQDs for moisture resistance. In view of previous investigations, we believe that the construction of SnS/CQDs heterojunctions might not only enhance the gas-sensitive response of pure SnS, but also achieve the goal of gas detection in high humidity environments.

In this work, to promote the gas-sensitive properties of the SnS-based sensor, nanocomposites of SnS/CQDs were constructed by modifying CQDs on the surface of SnS nanosheets via hydrothermal and stirring methods, respectively. In terms of morphology, it was found that a high density of CQDs particles were attached to the hydrothermally fabricated SnS/CQDs nanosheets, while the stirred sample was extremely similar to pure SnS with a lower density of CQDs particles on the nanosheets. Comparing pure SnS and SnS/CQDs composite formed via stirring method, the hydrothermally prepared composite of SnS/CQDs exhibits remarkable ammonia-sensitive properties at room temperature, including higher response, excellent sensitivity, and lower detection limit. More importantly, it also shows a unique humidity enhancement effect, as evidence that the response value increases with increasing humidity. Based on the heterojunction effect, we propose an ammonia-sensitive enhancement mechanism for SnS/CQDs composite. First-principles calculations show that pre-adsorbed H2O enhances the adsorption energy of NH3. The humidity-boosted NH3 adsorption-ionization model was further presented to explain the humidity enhancement effect. The present work proved the high effectiveness of CQDs modification on achieving stable detection of NH3 in high humidity environments.

High performance CQDs were obtained on the basis of the literature [36]. Typically, 3.152 g of citric acid and 1005 μl of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water under magnetic stirring. After stirring for 1 h, the homogeneous solution was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 5 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled naturally to 25 °C. Finally, the suspensions containing carbon quantum dots was obtained after 24 h of dialysis to remove unreacted materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pure SnS and nanocomposites of SnS/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported, pure SnS was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method [26]. To prepare CQDs-modified SnS, we attempted hydrothermal and stirring methods, respectively. In the hydrothermal method, different amounts of as-prepared CQDs suspensions (1 ml, 2 ml and 4 ml) were pre-introduced into the SnS solvothermal precursor solution, while other steps and materials remained unchanged. Noteworthy, the final powders can only be washed with anhydrous ethanol, because carbon quantum dots are extremely soluble in water. After drying, we obtained three SnS/CQDs samples with different contents of CQDs and labeled as SC-H1, SC-H2 and SC-H4, respectively. As for the stirring method, 0.1 g previously synthesized pure SnS and different amounts of CQDs suspensions (1 ml, 2 ml and 4 ml) were dispersed into 30 ml of ethanol solution under magnetic stirring for 60 min to form uniform suspensions. Then the wet precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with fresh ethanol for two times. After drying in air at 60 °C for 12 h, dry powders were achieved and the three samples were named as SC-S1, SC-S2 and SC-S4, sequentially. The schematic diagram of the corresponding synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The composition and crystalline structure of all products were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). The surface morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S4800) and field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, TECNAI G2F-20). Simultaneously, the elemental composition of samples was analyzed by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) during the testing of the FESEM system.

The gas-sensing performance of the pure SnS, SC-H and SC-S was assessed via using a home-made static volume gas-sensing system at 25 °C [37]. Before assessing, we first spin-coated the slurry of prepared powder samples (5:1 vol ratio of powder to ethanol) onto a clean ceramic substrate, which with 500 nm thick Au interdigital electrodes attachment to fabricate gas sensors. The final gas sensors are obtained by drying the spin-coated device in an infrared oven for 10 min. During gas-sensing test, the appropriate and accurate volume of ammonia liquid is instantaneously introduced into the test chamber through a micro-injector to get the desired concentration (0.1–10 ppm). The formulae for conversion of target gas concentration and liquid volume is as follows [38]:
(1)

22.4 ρ D

where C (ppm) is the concentration of the target gas, ρ (g/mL), D and M (g/mol) are the density, purity and relative molecular weight of the target liquid, respectively, and V 1(L) and V 2 (L) are the volume of the desired liquid and the test chamber, respectively.

The continuous recording for sensor resistance is realized by the NUI TUT70D professional digital multimeter, which is connected to the computer to preserve the recorded data [38]. The response of the sensor is defined by the following formula:
(2)
S =
R a
R g
where R a and R g represent the sensor resistance in air and target gas, respectively. The response and recovery times are defined as the time it takes for the sensor resistance to reach 90 % equilibrium after injection or removal of the target gas, respectively. Additionally, the response of the gas sensor under different humidity is further test, and the corresponding test details are described in the supporting information.

The first-principles calculations on the energy of the SnS, CQDs together with the corresponding gas adsorption systems were carried out based on density functional theory (DFT) [39]. The Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) in Materials Studio of Accelrys Inc is used throughout the calculations. The energy exchange correlation energy function adopted the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) parameterized by Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) to generate a suitable fundamental structure of the adsorption system [40]. To ensure the accuracy of the calculation results, we set the plane-wave expansion cutoff energy to 400 eV, the energy convergence range to 1 × 10−5 eV/atom, and the Brillouin zone (BZ) integrals of all models are sampled by a 1 × 3 × 3 k-grid [41]. In addition, to reduce the interaction of adjacent layers in the z-direction, the vacuum space was set to 20 Å. All calculations were performed in the reciprocal space.

The SEM photographs shown in Fig. 2 (a)–(d) display the morphology and microstructure of the pure SnS, SC-H2 and SC-S2. Fig. 2(a) and (b) reveal that the typical three-dimensional (3D) flower-like morphology of pure SnS is assembled by stacked massive 2D nanosheets with nuances. Fig. 2(c) indicates that SC-H2 has a looser structure compared to pure SnS, which will facilitate gas adsorption–desorption. Importantly, granular particles with high distribution density are observed to attach SnS nanosheets, which implies that carbon quantum dots were successfully deposited and modified on the surface of SnS. In Fig. 2(d), the sample of SC-S2 shows extremely similar morphology with pure SnS, with low density of particles attached on the nanosheets appearing in the field of view. Evidently, stirring-induced distribution of carbon quantum dots on the SnS surface is relatively sparse.

To further investigate the internal micromorphology and lattice structure of the SnS/CQDs nanocomposites, we carried out TEM characterization of the SC-H2 samples at different magnifications. The low magnification TEM image (Fig. 3 (a)) clearly displays the presence of numerous spherical particles on the surface of the SnS nanosheets, demonstrating the successful synthesis of SnS/CQDs nanocomposites. It is more clearly distinguishable in the higher magnification of Fig. 3(b) that the carbon quantum dots are uniformly distributed on the surface and edges of the nanosheets, forming a tight heterogeneous structure. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the particle size of CQDs mostly ranges from of 2–4 nm, with an average particle size of 3.46 nm. In addition, the HRTEM image of SnS/CQDs in Fig. 3(d) further displays the clear lattice spacing of SnS and CQDs. As seen, the resolvable lattice spacing of 0.278 nm perfectly matches the (040) crystal plane of the orthogonal SnS [42], while the interlayer stripe spacing of 0.324 nm is identified as the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [43]. The EDS elemental mapping of Fig. 3(e–h) display the elemental composition and spatial distribution of the SC-H2 samples. Obviously, the C elements of CQDs and the Sn and S elements of SnS are uniformly distributed throughout the products, verifying the good dispersion characteristics of CQDs and SnS in CQDs-modified SnS.

In order to in-depth analysis the crystal structures of the samples, the total XRD patterns of pure SnS, SC-H2 and SC-S2 are shown in Fig. 4 . As seen, the distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ of 21.98°, 25.97°, 27.44°, 30.47°, 31.48°and 39.06° can correspond to the (110), (120), (021), (101), (111) and (131) planes of orthogonal SnS (JCPDS No. 39-0354), respectively [44]. Note that the XRD patterns of SC-H2 and SC-S2 are very similar to that of pure SnS. No characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs were observed in all XRD patterns, which may be related to the relatively low content of CQDs in the composites [36,45]. It illustrates that the present content of CQDs will not affect the synthesis of SnS.

Fig. 5 (a) depicts the response values of seven sensors at 25 °C and 25 % RH for 0.1–10 ppm NH3, which include pure SnS, three hydrothermal samples and three stirred samples of SnS/CQDs with different CQDs contents (i.e., SC-S1, SC-S2, SC-S4; SC-H1, SC-H2, SC-H4, respectively). Pure SnS shows a tenuous response to NH3 with a response value of only 1.17 for 10 ppm NH3. Comparatively, the sensors based on SnS/CQDs composites show significantly enhanced capability for NH3 sensing. Specifically, the response values of SC-S2 and SC-H2 to 10 ppm NH3 are 4.42 and 5.72, respectively, which are 3.78 and 4.89 times of that of pure SnS. The higher ammonia-sensitivity of SC-H2 sensor than the SC-S2 might be associated with the higher distribution density of CQDs on the SnS/CQDs nanosheets prepared by the hydrothermal method than on the stirred samples. It is also clear that the NH3 response of SC-H2 is generally higher than that of SC-H1 or SC-H4, indicating that the response enhancement effect of NH3 is directly related to the composition ratio of CQDs and SnS in the composite. In this work, the SnS/CQDs nanocomposites achieved with 2 ml of CQDs solution display the maximum response to NH3 vapor.

Fig. S2 (a) and Fig. 5(b) show the real-time dynamic response curves of the SC-S2 and SC-H2 sensors to 0.1–10 ppm NH3 at room temperature, respectively. As obtained from the graph, the resistance values of both sensors drop abruptly after exposure to NH3, exhibiting a typical n-type semiconductor characteristic. As the NH3 concentration increased from 0.1 to 10 ppm, the response value of SC-S2 increases from 1.11 to 4.42, while for the SC-H2 sensor, the response amplitude increases rapidly from 1.26 to 5.72. Remarkably, the response value of SC-H2 towards 100 ppb NH3 is even higher than that of pure SnS to 10 ppm NH3 (1.17), indicating that the SC-H2 sensor has the ability to detect trace amounts of NH3. Further LOD calculations show that the SC-H2 sensor can theoretically sense as low as 6.73 ppb NH3, which is lower than the LOD (33.58 ppb) of SC-S2, illustrating the great potential of SC-H2 nanocomposites in detection of ultra-low concentration NH3. The detailed calculation procedure is described in the supporting information. In Fig. 5(c) and Fig. S2(b), the reversibility of SC-H2 and SC-S2 to 2.5 ppm NH3 in four consecutive cycles was further checked. The response values of these four consecutive cycles are very similar, with the average errors of 0.88 % and 1.63 % for SC-S2 and SC-H2 sensors, respectively, hinting that the SC-H2 sensor has better stability.

We also evaluated the selectivity of pure SnS, SC-S2 and SC-H2. The response values of the three sensors to 10 ppm NH3 and five other interfering gases (methanol, isopropanol (IPA), toluene, acetone and ethanol) at 25 % RH and 25 °C were studied as shown in Fig. 5(d). The response values of pure SnS sensor to six gases are very low. While the SC-H2 sensor shows a much larger response towards NH3 than the other five gases, which indicates that the sensor has a certain discriminatory ability in detecting NH3 and other interfering gases. Fig. 5(e) describes the instantaneous response and recovery times of 7 s and 103 s for SC-H2 sensor towards 10 ppm NH3, respectively. The fast response and recovery characteristics of the SC-H2 sensor facilitate practical applications in gas detection. As another key parameter, the long-term stability of SC-H2 was evaluated by monitoring the response of the sensor to 2.5 ppm NH3 every 3 days for 21 days. Fig. 5(f) shows the seven dynamic response curves of the sensor during the test cycle. The maximum deviation of the sensor response value was 8.62 % over 21 days, proving the acceptable stability of the present SnS/CQDs sensor.

The excellent capability of the SnS/CQDs sensor in NH3 detection could be further reflected by performing the performance comparison between the SC-H2 sensor of this work and the previously reported NH3 sensors, as listed in Table S3. Compared with the reported sensors, the present SC-H2 sensor exhibits superior characteristics in terms of response value, response recovery time and operating temperature, indicating the potential practicability of the fabricated SnS/CQDs nanocomposites for high-performance NH3 sensors.

Generally, the sensitive performance of gas sensors is significantly affected by ambient humidity. To explore the ability of our sensor operating in high humidity environments, we tested the dynamic response of the SC-H2 sensor to 2.5 ppm NH3 under different humidity conditions (25 %–97 % RH), as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Clearly, the SC-H2 sensor exhibits a distinctive humidity enhancement effect. As shown, the sensor maintains a relatively stable response below 40 % RH. With the humidity increasing to 80 % RH, the response value corresponds to increase from 2.76 to 3.59, showing an increase of 30.07 %. Under the extremely high humidity of 97 % RH, the response proceeds to 3.67, which is 1.44 times of the value of 25 % RH. The response values of NH3 significantly enhanced with increasing humidity are shown in Fig. 6(b). Obviously, the humidity enhancement effect of the SC-H2 sensor promotes the excellent NH3 sensing characteristics under high humidity conditions, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of NH3 and pre-adsorbed H2O on the material surface. In Fig. 6(b), the baseline resistance of SC-H2 at different humidity levels are also shown. As displayed, the baseline resistance of the sensor plunged as the humidity increase, indicating that water molecules can significantly affect the sensor performance. Noteworthy, to exclude the effect of water molecules on the sensor performance during the gas-sensing tests, the target gas was not introduced into the test chamber until the sensor achieves stable value of baseline resistance. Furthermore, to investigate the effect of humidity on sensor selectivity, we tested the response values of SC-H2 to different gases under both dry and high humidity conditions. The response values of the sensor towards 2.5 ppm of methanol, isopropanol (IPA), toluene, acetone, ethanol and NH3 at 25 % RH and 97 % RH are shown in Fig. 6(c). Compared to other interfering gases, the SC-H2 sensor shows the highest response to NH3 at both humidity conditions. To quantify the selectivity difference, we define a selectivity factor K as the ratio of response value of interfering gas to NH3 [46]:
(3)
K =
S
gas
S
Ammonia
where S gas and S Ammonia are the response values towards the interfering gas and NH3 gas, respectively. K 1 and K 2 represent the selectivity factors of the sensor to different gases at 25 % RH and 97 % RH, respectively, and are plotted in Fig. 6(d). As seen, the calculated K value for the SC-H2 sensor towards other gases is much smaller than that of NH3. The smaller K values imply higher interference tolerance, indicating that the sensor has higher selectivity to NH3 than other interfering gases. Notably, the K values of the five gases at 97 % RH is less than those at 25 % RH, expressing that the SC-H2 sensor has a superior NH3 selection capability under high humidity conditions. Most notably, despite the higher water solubility of ethanol, acetone, and methanol than NH3, the response of the device to these gases hardly changed with increasing humidity, indicating that the NH3-specific recognition of SC-H2 is the essential reason for the significantly improved performance.

In this work, the ammonia-sensitive enhancement mechanism of the SnS/CQDs sensor is elaborated in terms of heterogeneous synergistic effects and the corresponding conduction pathways, as shown in Fig. 7 . Here, pure SnS is a p-type semiconductor [47,48], while both the synthesized SnS/CQDs and CQDs exhibit typical n-type semiconductor properties, as shown in Fig. 5(b) and Fig. S3. In addition, the work functions of SnS and CQDs are 4.28 eV and 5.29 eV, respectively [42,49,50]. The corresponding energy band diagrams for both in vacuum without contact is shown in Fig. 7(a). When p-type SnS and n-type CQDs are contacted in air, the difference of their work functions leads to the transfer of electrons from SnS to CQDs during EF equilibrium. It forms a hole accumulation layer (HAL) and an electron accumulation layer (EAL) at the interface near SnS and CQDs, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Based on the morphology of CQDs densely covered on the surface of the SnS nanosheets shown in Fig. 3(b) and the n-type response characteristics exhibited for SnS/CQDs in the NH3 environment, we believe that the oxidation–reduction reaction of NH3 occurs mainly on the surface of the CQDs. Hence, the conductivity variations of the composite SnS/CQDs are primarily determined by the CQDs and the highly conductive EAL. Furthermore, more active sites for NH3 adsorption are provided by functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots. When exposed to reducing NH3, the conductive pathway EAL will be rapidly expanded owing to the adsorption of NH3 molecules onto the surface of the CQDs to inject electrons into the sensitive material, as shown in Fig. 7(c) and (f).

In air (T < 100 °C), oxygen molecules, as strong oxidizers, are easily adsorbed on the surface of sensitive films, extracting electrons from the conduction band and forming O2 − [51], as follow formulas. Compared to pure SnS, SnS/CQDs will tend to form more O2 − owing to the heterojunction between SnS and CQDs as shown in Fig. 7(d) and (e).

When exposed to NH3, the NH3 molecules are adsorbed and react with the pre-adsorbed O2 − [52] as demonstrated in Eqs. (6) and (7), releasing the electrons trapped by O2 − back to the sensitive material.

For the SnS-based sensor, the released electrons diffuse into the HAL of the SnS surface, resulting in a slightly reduced HAL channel as shown in Fig. 7(d). More NH3 molecules will react with the adsorbed O2 − and inject more electrons into the SnS/CQDs material because the surface of the SnS/CQDs has more O2 − than SnS. It results in the dramatic expansion of the EAL near the heterogeneous interface due to the rapid diffusion of electrons into the EAL (Fig. 7(c) and (f)). Therefore, the resistance of the composite decreases rapidly in NH3 atmosphere, as revealed in the experimental test in Fig. 5(b). As shown in Fig. 7(d) and (f), the SnS/CQDs shows much larger change in conductive channel in NH3 environment than pure SnS implying that the sensor has an ultra-high gas sensitive response to NH3.

The gas sensor based on SnS/CQDs composite exhibits excellent sensing performance under high humidity conditions, which is inseparable from the synergistic effect of water molecules and NH3. Here, we present a humidity-boosted NH3 adsorption-ionization model to elaborate the humidity enhancement effect, as shown in Fig. 8 .

Under relatively low humidity (20–40 % RH), fewer water molecules are adsorbed on the surface of sensitive layer. In this case, the response of the device to NH3 is mainly attributed to the electron transfer induced by the reaction between NH3 and the oxygen ions adsorbed. With humidity increasing (40 %–80 % RH), H2O molecules are chemisorbed onto the surface of the SnS/CQDs sensitive layer through hydrogen bonds because of the presence of hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on the surface of CQDs [53,54]. In the ambient with moderate humidity ambient, a discontinuous water film could be formed [55]. It causes more NH3 molecules to adsorb on the surface of the sensitive film, owing to the hydrogen bonding between NH3 and H2O molecules. At this time, other than direct charge transfer cause by NH3 adsorption and reaction, hydrogen bonding also induces additional charge transfer with the sensitive layer, which leads to a rapid decrease in resistance.

When the humidity reaches 80 %–97 % RH, more H2O molecules are physically adsorbed on the surface of the water molecule layer through hydrogen bonds to form a continuous water film [56]. Water molecules spontaneously ionize H+ and OH−, and the H+ is protonated with another H2O to form H3O+, which is involved in conduction according to the Grotthuss mechanism[54]:

Upon exposure to NH3, it will easily form NH4 + and OH− in water film, as described by equation (10) [57], because NH3 is extremely soluble in water with a solubility of 1:700. And OH− will react with the carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs to form RCOO− (equation (11)), thus enhancing the conductivity of sensitive films. On the other hand, H3O+ and NH4 + can be rapidly transferred over the continuous water layer.

Obviously, the influence of both factors can promote the decrease in the resistance of the sensor. In summary, the humidity enhancement effect plays a very important role for SnS/CQDs sensor to detect NH3 in high humidity environments.

The effect of H2O pre-adsorbed on the surface of CQDs on NH3 sensing process was further analyzed based on first-principles calculations. We first separately established the computational models for the adsorption of NH3 and H2O on SnS[40] and CQDs[58], respectively. To ensure the effectiveness and rigor of the calculation, we selected seven different adsorption locations based on the charge density difference map of CQDs in Fig. 9 (a). Fig. 9(b) and (c) summarize the adsorption energies (E ads) of all possible structures of SnS and CQDs for NH3 and H2O adsorption and exhibits the most stable adsorption model of CQDs for NH3 and H2O. Here, the E ads of SnS and CQDs for NH3 and H2O molecules adsorption are calculated as follows:

where ENH3, ECQDs (SnS) and E CQDs (SnS)+NH3 correspond to the energy of individual NH3 molecule, the substrate (CQDs or SnS) and the whole adsorption system, respectively.

It is particularly noted that the final results of the Eads for CQDs to NH3 and H2O are only ten and six outcomes, respectively, instead of fourteen. Because after optimization, NH3 and H2O molecules are transferred to form a more stable model. The most stable adsorption model of SnS for NH3 is Sn-N shown in Fig. S4(b), with a corresponding E ads of 0.152 eV. Comparatively, the adsorption energies of H2-N and H3-N on NH3 are almost the same with about 0.55 eV, which is 3.62-fold compared to that of SnS. We speculate that it is OH as a whole that acts on the adsorption of NH3. The above results reveal that CQDs have a stronger ability to capture NH3 than SnS, indicating that the CQDs-modified composites have an enhanced NH3-sensing response. From Fig. 9(c), it can be seen that the maximum E ads of H2O on CQDs is 0.52 eV, which corresponds to the model of H3-O, while the Eads of H2O on SnS is only 0.11 eV, with the corresponding model shown in Fig. S4(c). The results show that the CQDs have stronger hydrophilicity than SnS. Then, four models were built in which NH3 was placed near the H2O and the corresponding adsorption energies are shown in Fig. 9(d). The adsorption energy of NH3 on CQDs with water pre-adsorption is defined as:

where ECQDs+ H2O+NH3 corresponds to the energy of NH3 molecule on CQDs with water pre-adsorbed. The optimal adsorption configuration of NH3 on CQDs with pre-absorbed water is that both H of H2O and N of NH3 are adsorbed on O3 and H3 of CQDs (O3-H H3-N), respectively. At this time, the corresponding Eads is 0.726 eV, which is larger than the Eads without water pre-adsorbed (0.549 eV). Therefore, the pre-adsorption of H2O has an enhanced effect on the NH3 sensing performance, which is consistent with the experimental phenomenon. Taken together, the DFT calculations further clarify that the high performance of the SnS/CQDs sensor for NH3 in high humidity environments is attributed to the synergistic effect of NH3 and H2O.

In summary, we successfully prepared CQDs-modified SnS nanocomposites by hydrothermal and stirring methods, respectively. The morphology of SnS/CQDs obtained by the stirring method is similar to that of pure SnS, while the hydrothermally prepared SnS/CQDs nanosheets are attached with a high distribution density of CQDs. Thus, the hydrothermally prepared SnS/CQDs composite exhibits excellent ammonia-sensitive properties at room temperature, including a higher response, excellent sensitivity and lower detection limit, which are attributed to the heterogeneous structure of SnS/CQDs. More importantly, the SnS/CQDs sensor displays a special humidity enhancement effect, which is inextricably linked to the synergistic interaction between H2O and NH3 molecules, as confirmed by first-principles calculations. Furthermore, a humidity-boosted NH3 adsorption-ionization model is proposed to explain it. Above all, the CQDs-modified SnS gas sensor designed in this work provides an effective strategy for the development of NH3 detection in high humidity environments.
